hero_image.alt: Portrait of W. E. B. Du Bois, 1907
hero_image.caption: W. E. B. Du Bois in 1907, four years after The Souls of Black Folk appeared.
hero_image.license: Public domain; NPG digital image released CC0

inline_images.alt: African American laborers operating a cotton gin and pressing baled cotton at Dahomey, Mississippi, 1899
inline_images.caption: A Mississippi cotton gin at Dahomey in 1899, the machinery and labor at the heart of the cotton economy Du Bois traces through the crop-lien debt that bound the tenant farmer.
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (digital ID ppmsca.53149)

inline_images.alt: Men gathered on the porch of a rural country store with gasoline pumps on a dirt road, Gordonton, North Carolina, 1939
inline_images.caption: A Southern country store in Gordonton, North Carolina, the kind of plantation-and-crossroads store where the crop-lien system ran tenant farmers into the perpetual debt Du Bois describes.
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (FSA/OWI Collection, digital ID fsa.8b33922)

related_chapters.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
related_chapters.chapter_title: You Owed Your Soul to the Store
related_chapters.why: Du Bois anatomizes the crop-lien debt that bound the cotton tenant; Blake describes the sharecropping debt system in which a family owed its soul to the plantation store.

related_chapters.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
related_chapters.why: The essay studies the Black Belt tenant working another man's cotton on shares; Jones recounts farming cotton, corn, and peas for a one-fourth share.

related_chapters.subject: Carrie Lamar Young
related_chapters.chapter_title: Picking Cotton, and the Weighing Scale
related_chapters.why: Du Bois walks the cotton-field golden fleece and the scales that set the tenant's pay; Young recounts picking cotton and the weighing scale in the Delta fields.

related_chapters.subject: Mary Jones
related_chapters.why: The essay notes children pulled into the cotton harvest in place of schooling; Jones describes the child farm labor of picking cotton and pulling corn.

related_chapters.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Only Address I Ever Had
related_chapters.why: Du Bois traces lives spent in cabins on another man's plantation; Clark describes growing up in a house on the plantation that was the only address he ever had.

related_chapters.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
related_chapters.chapter_title: Picked Them Up in the 1870 Census
related_chapters.why: The essay closes on the rare Black freeholder who climbs from tenancy to owning land; Sherrod recounts a landowning farm family traceable in the 1870 census.

top_voice.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
top_voice.reason: Blake's account of the sharecropping debt that left a family owing its soul to the store is the closest living echo of the essay's argument about the crop-lien economy.

closing_image.alt: Composite plate of five views of Bishop College in Marshall, Texas: Bishop Hall girls' dormitory, the president's residence and pool, the campus entrance gate, Rockefeller Hall, and Marston Hall boys' dormitory.
closing_image.caption: Composite plate of five views of Bishop College in Marshall, Texas: Bishop Hall girls' dormitory, the president's residence and pool, the campus entrance gate, Rockefeller Hall, and Marston Hall boys' dormitory. Reverend Harry Blake is the corpus voice this essay connects to most.
closing_image.repository: The National Cyclopedia of the Colored Race, ed. Clement Richardson (Montgomery: National Publishing Company, 1919)

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions Of the Quest of the Golden Fleece raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of Esther M. A. Terry, Lawrence Guyot, and Vernon Dahmer, Jr..

nearest_voices.subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
